audit_trail.name: Greyscale Conversion
audit_trail.description: Converted BGR input to single-channel greyscale. Colour is irrelevant for ink-on-paper OCR and removing it reduces processing cost by 3×.

audit_trail.name: Bilateral Denoising Filter
audit_trail.description: Applied edge-aware bilateral filter (d=9, σColor=75, σSpace=75). Suppresses paper grain and scanner noise while preserving the sharp ink-stroke boundaries that OCR depends on.

audit_trail.name: CLAHE Contrast Enhancement
audit_trail.description: Applied Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (clipLimit=2.0, tile=8×8) to recover faded ink strokes and compensate for lighting gradients across the document surface.

audit_trail.name: Adaptive Gaussian Binarization
audit_trail.description: Converted to binary using local Gaussian-weighted thresholding (blockSize=15, C=10). Each pixel is compared to its 15×15 local neighbourhood average, isolating ink strokes from yellow staining and uneven lighting that would defeat global thresholding.

audit_trail.name: OCR — Tesseract LSTM (PSM 6, OEM 3)
audit_trail.description: Recognised text at 49% mean confidence. 29% of tokens high-confidence (≥80%). Tokens below 40% confidence dropped from extracted_text to eliminate scanner-border noise.

audit_trail.name: Metadata Extraction
audit_trail.description: Regex entity extraction found 0 date(s), 3 person(s), 1 place(s). Document type: unrecognised.

audit_trail.name: Annotated Image
audit_trail.description: Drew per-word bounding boxes colour-coded green/yellow/red by OCR confidence tier (≥80 / 50-79 / <50).

audit_trail.name: Layout Map
audit_trail.description: Morphological dilation (kernel 15×5, 2 iterations) merged characters into paragraph blobs; contours mark layout regions.

audit_trail.name: Table Detection
audit_trail.description: Morphological line extraction + numeric-alignment heuristic. Found 1 table(s). Note: borderless tables require deep-learning models (future work).

numeric_facts: Photograph by ReJLANDER. Proofs, Prints, 103. d. 4 sy

ai_analysis.artifact_profile: This document appears to be a printed publisher's catalog or advertisement sheet, likely produced on paper with ink. The OCR quality is poor, characterized by significant character errors, fragmented words, and noise, suggesting a heavily weathered or complex original substrate.
ai_analysis.provenance:
- Estimated Time Period: Mid-to-late 19th century (based on references to the "Late Prince Consort" and early photographic processes)
- Geographic Origin: Edinburgh, Scotland
- Author or Creator: James Wood (Publisher)
- Document Genre: Commercial/Advertising
ai_analysis.anthropological_matrix: The author occupies a commercial position within a capitalist framework, acting as an intermediary between creators and the consuming public. The intended audience includes tourists, photography enthusiasts, and scholars interested in science and art. Dominant dynamics include the rise of technological bureaucracy (photography and chemistry) and the commodification of landscape and celebrity. Notable silences include the working-class individuals in the "Old Town" or "Quarry" views and the manual laborers involved in the production of these printed goods.
ai_analysis.taxonomy:
- Locations: Edinburgh, Calton Hill, Edinburgh Castle, Princes Street, Craigleith Quarry, Loch Katrine, Ben Venue, Loch Achray, Ross, Liverpool, London
- People: James Wood, The Late Prince Consort, Rejlander
- Organizations: London Morning Advertiser, Liverpool Photographic Journal, The Photographic News, The Photographic Times
- Thematic Tags: Economics, Technology (photography, chemistry, heliochromy), Tourism, Art, Science
ai_analysis.validity_bias: The document is a commercial advertisement and therefore possesses a high level of overt bias intended to promote sales. It is most valuable for understanding the consumer culture, technological interests, and publishing industry of the 19th-century Victorian era.
ai_analysis.executive_summary: This document is a 19th-century publisher's catalog from James Wood in Edinburgh, advertising various books, photographic journals, and scenic views. It is significant for social science research as it illustrates the intersection of emerging photographic technology, the commercialization of Scottish landscapes, and the Victorian publishing industry.
